questions.question: Define the joint probability mass function (PMF) for two discrete random variables X and Y. State and explain the two fundamental properties that any valid joint PMF must satisfy.
keyPoints: Definition: p_{X,Y}(x,y) = P(X=x, Y=y), the probability that X takes the value x and Y takes the value y simultaneously.
Non-negativity: p_{X,Y}(x,y) ≥ 0 for all possible values (x, y) in the sample space.
Normalization: The sum of p_{X,Y}(x,y) over all possible pairs (x, y) must equal 1. That is, ∑_{x} ∑_{y} p_{X,Y}(x,y) = 1.

questions.question: Given the joint PMF of two discrete random variables X and Y, describe the process of obtaining the marginal PMF of X. Then, explain how to determine if X and Y are independent random variables, stating the precise mathematical condition that must hold for all x and y.
keyPoints: The marginal PMF p_{X}(x) is found by summing the joint PMF over all possible values of Y: p_{X}(x) = ∑_{y} p_{X,Y}(x,y). This 'sums out' the influence of Y.
X and Y are independent if and only if the joint PMF factors into the product of the marginal PMFs for every possible pair (x,y).
The mathematical condition for independence is: p_{X,Y}(x,y) = p_{X}(x) * p_{Y}(y) for all x and y.

questions.question: A simple computer network has two states: 0 (idle) and 1 (active). Let X_n be the state at time n. The network's behavior is modeled by the joint probabilities: P(X_n=0, X_{n+1}=0) = 0.4, P(X_n=0, X_{n+1}=1) = 0.2, P(X_n=1, X_{n+1}=0) = 0.1, P(X_n=1, X_{n+1}=1) = 0.3. First, find the marginal distribution of X_n (the initial state). Then, calculate the conditional probability P(X_{n+1}=1 | X_n=0). Finally, based on your calculations, discuss whether this model demonstrates the Markov property and justify your reasoning.
keyPoints: The marginal PMF of X_n is found by summing the joint PMF over all possible values of X_{n+1}: p_{X_n}(x) = ∑_{y} p_{X_n,X_{n+1}}(x,y).
The conditional probability P(X_{n+1}=1 | X_n=0) is calculated using the definition of conditional probability: P(X_{n+1}=1 | X_n=0) = P(X_n=0, X_{n+1}=1) / P(X_n=0).
The Markov property is demonstrated if the conditional probability P(X_{n+1}=1 | X_n=0) depends only on the current state X_n and not on any previous states.
